---
title: "DEEP-SEAM four-region transferability test"
subtitle: "A controlled 2x2 experiment across Curnamona REE, Tanacross porphyry-Cu, Arizona porphyry-Cu, and Lawley continental MVT"
date: 2026-05-11
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: true
execute:
  warning: false
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

import json
from pathlib import Path

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

DATA_DERIVED = Path("/home/sky/src/learning/ai-minerals/data/derived")
```

## What this experiment tests

DEEP-SEAM (Luo et al. 2026, *Geoscientific Model Development*) is a
recent deep-anomaly-detection architecture for mineral prospectivity.
On a curated Curnamona Province REE dataset with seven known
deposits, the published version of the architecture beat Random
Forest by a factor of two to three on top-k capture rates. The
question this experiment asks is whether that architectural
advantage transfers from the dataset it was tuned on to standard
regional prospectivity workflows on different deposit types and
different feature stacks.

The architecture uses a "deviation loss" that pushes positive cells
to have scores far above a Gaussian-distributed reference. The
loss assumes positive cells are anomalies in feature space — that
the geological signal we want to predict is a rare-outlier signature
rather than a common-recipe signature. Whether that assumption holds
across deposit types is the central question.

## The 2x2 experimental design

For each region, the experiment crosses model architecture against
data preprocessing:

|   | DevNet (DEEP-SEAM architecture) | Random Forest |
|---|---|---|
| **DEEP-SEAM data style** (ILR-PCA on geochem + GLCM textural features on geophysics) | Cell A | Cell B |
| **Our data style** (raw public-data feature stack, no decomposition) | Cell C | Cell D |

The same evaluation method runs across cells:
spatial-block cross-validation with 20-km blocks, leak-free
out-of-fold scoring, every cell evaluated by a model that did not
train on its spatial block.

## The four regions tested

| Region | Commodity | Positives | Cells | Tectonic setting | Deposit homogeneity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Curnamona Province** (S. Australia) | rare earth elements | 7 | ~870 train / ~93k pred | Single Proterozoic IOCG-style province | High (single deposit type, single event) |
| **Tanacross / Eastern Alaska** | porphyry Cu-Mo | 45 | ~270k | Mixed metallogenic history (100+ Myr) | Low (Cu, Mo, distal porphyry, polymetallic veins all in the cleaned label set) |
| **Southeast Arizona porphyry belt** | porphyry Cu | 191 | ~98k | Single Laramide event (66-74 Ma) | High (single deposit type, single event) |
| **Lawley continental MVT** (US + Canada + Australia) | sediment-hosted Zn-Pb | 2,027 | ~5.16M | Two continents, multiple sedimentary basins | Single deposit type |

The four regions span four deposit types, three continents, two
orders of magnitude in positive count, and the full range of
deposit-homogeneity from "single event, single deposit type" to
"multi-event, multi-deposit-type." If the architectural advantage is
real, it should hold across at least some of these regions. If it
holds only on the dataset it was tuned on, that tells us something
specific about the dataset.

## The four-region capture-at-top-1% comparison

```{python}
#| label: cross-region-table
#| code-fold: true
rows = [
    # Curnamona: cell A & B are leave-one-out (LOO) on 7 positives
    {'Region': 'Curnamona REE (LOO, 7 pos)',
     'RF raw': '—',     'DevNet raw': '—',
     'RF decomposed': '0%',  'DevNet decomposed': '43%',
     'Winner': 'DevNet (matches publication)'},
    {'Region': 'Tanacross porphyry-Cu (OOF, 45 pos)',
     'RF raw': '22.2%', 'DevNet raw': '13.3%',
     'RF decomposed': '15.6%', 'DevNet decomposed': '13.3%',
     'Winner': 'RF on raw features'},
    {'Region': 'Arizona porphyry-Cu (OOF, 191 pos)',
     'RF raw': '23.0%', 'DevNet raw': '8.9%',
     'RF decomposed': '14.7%', 'DevNet decomposed': '13.6%',
     'Winner': 'RF on raw features'},
    {'Region': 'Lawley continental MVT (2-D blocked OOF, 2,027 pos)',
     'RF raw': '28.4%', 'DevNet raw': '0.0%',
     'RF decomposed': '(not run)', 'DevNet decomposed': '(not run)',
     'Winner': 'GBM, by 28 percentage points'},
]
pd.DataFrame(rows)
```

The table reads as out-of-fold capture at the top 1 percent of
cells: the fraction of held-out positive deposits that fall inside
the top 1 percent of cells the model ranks most prospective.

The headline pattern is clear. On Curnamona — the dataset DEEP-SEAM
was tuned on — the deviation network beats Random Forest by a factor
of 2 or more. On every other region tested, classical tree-based
methods (Random Forest or gradient-boosted trees on the Lawley
benchmark) beat DevNet by a wide margin. The widest margin is on
the Lawley continental MVT benchmark, where the gradient-boosted-
trees model lands 28 percent of held-out deposits in its top 1
percent of cells and DevNet lands **none**.

## What each cell of the experiment shows

### Cell A: DevNet on DEEP-SEAM-style preprocessed data — reproducing the publication

```{python}
#| label: cell-a-curnamona
#| code-fold: true
ca = json.loads((DATA_DERIVED / 'deep_seam_cell_a_loo.json').read_text())
cap = ca['capture_at_top_k']
print("Curnamona LOO, Cell A (DevNet + DEEP-SEAM preprocessing):")
print(f"  Top 1% capture: {cap['top_1_pct']['rate']*100:.1f}% ({cap['top_1_pct']['captured']}/7 positives)")
print(f"  Top 2% capture: {cap['top_2_pct']['rate']*100:.1f}%")
print(f"  Top 5% capture: {cap['top_5_pct']['rate']*100:.1f}%")
print(f"  Top 30% capture: {cap['top_30_pct']['rate']*100:.1f}%")
```

We reproduced the published DEEP-SEAM Curnamona result from the
methods section alone, without consulting the authors' code. The
top 1% capture matches the published figure within a few percentage
points: three of seven positives in the top 1 percent of cells
under leave-one-out evaluation, six of seven in the top 5 percent.
The architecture genuinely works on this dataset.

### Cell B: Random Forest on the same DEEP-SEAM-preprocessed data

```{python}
#| label: cell-b-curnamona
#| code-fold: true
cb = json.loads((DATA_DERIVED / 'deep_seam_cell_b_loo.json').read_text())
cap = cb['capture_at_top_k']
print("Curnamona LOO, Cell B (RF + DEEP-SEAM preprocessing):")
print(f"  Top 1% capture: {cap['top_1_pct']['rate']*100:.1f}% ({cap['top_1_pct']['captured']}/7 positives)")
print(f"  Top 5% capture: {cap['top_5_pct']['rate']*100:.1f}%")
print(f"  Top 30% capture: {cap['top_30_pct']['rate']*100:.1f}%")
```

The same Random Forest baseline we use on regional prospectivity
workflows, scored against the DEEP-SEAM-preprocessed Curnamona data.
The architectural advantage from Cell A is real: DevNet's 43 percent
top-1% beats RF's near-zero. On Curnamona, DEEP-SEAM is genuinely
the better architecture.

### Cell C and D: switching to a regional MPM workflow on Tanacross + Arizona

Both regions show the inversion. On Tanacross, RF on raw features
captures 22.2 percent of held-out positives at top-1 percent; DevNet
captures 13.3 percent. On Arizona, RF captures 23.0 percent; DevNet
captures 8.9 percent. The DEEP-SEAM preprocessing helps DevNet
modestly on both regions (Arizona: 8.9 → 13.6 percent) but hurts RF
substantially (Arizona: 23.0 → 14.7 percent).

```{python}
#| label: tanacross-arizona-comparison
#| code-fold: true
#| fig-cap: "Tanacross and Arizona out-of-fold capture-at-top-k curves. Both regions show RF (orange family) beating DevNet (blue family) on raw features, with the DEEP-SEAM preprocessing helping DevNet a little and hurting RF a lot."
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 6))
percents = [1, 2, 5, 10, 30]
x = np.array(percents) / 100

# Tanacross
tana = json.loads((DATA_DERIVED / 'eastak' / 'path5_decomposed_metrics.json').read_text())
ax = axes[0]
for key, color, label in [
    ('RF_v3p3', '#cc6600', 'RF on DEEP-SEAM preprocessed'),
    ('DevNet_v3p3', '#003366', 'DevNet on DEEP-SEAM preprocessed'),
]:
    cap = tana[key]['capture_at_top_k']
    ys = [cap[f'top_{p}_pct']['rate'] for p in percents]
    ax.plot(x, ys, '-o', color=color, label=label, lw=2, markersize=7)
ax.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], '--', color='#888', label='random', lw=1)
ax.set_xlabel("Fraction of area (top k%)")
ax.set_ylabel("Fraction of held-out positives captured")
ax.set_xscale('log')
ax.set_xlim(0.008, 0.5); ax.set_ylim(0, 1)
ax.set_title("Tanacross (45 positives, OOF)")
ax.legend(loc='lower right', fontsize=9)
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

# Arizona
ariz = json.loads((DATA_DERIVED / 'arizona' / 'path3_decomposed_metrics.json').read_text())
ax = axes[1]
for key, color, label in [
    ('RF_raw',           '#cc6600', 'RF on raw features'),
    ('RF_decomposed',    '#ff9933', 'RF on DEEP-SEAM preprocessed'),
    ('DevNet_raw',       '#003366', 'DevNet on raw features'),
    ('DevNet_decomposed', '#3366aa', 'DevNet on DEEP-SEAM preprocessed'),
]:
    cap = ariz[key]['capture_at_top_k']
    ys = [cap[f'top_{p}_pct']['rate'] for p in percents]
    ax.plot(x, ys, '-o', color=color, label=label, lw=2, markersize=7)
ax.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], '--', color='#888', label='random', lw=1)
ax.set_xlabel("Fraction of area (top k%)")
ax.set_ylabel("Fraction of held-out positives captured")
ax.set_xscale('log')
ax.set_xlim(0.008, 0.5); ax.set_ylim(0, 1)
ax.set_title("Arizona (191 positives, OOF)")
ax.legend(loc='lower right', fontsize=8.5)
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

## What the experiment establishes

### Three hypotheses ruled out

The experiment was designed to test three specific hypotheses about
why the DEEP-SEAM architecture might or might not transfer to
regional MPM. All three turn out to be wrong in the same direction.

**Hypothesis 1: deposit homogeneity is the gap.** The original
DEEP-SEAM dataset has seven REE deposits in a single Proterozoic
IOCG-style province. Tanacross has 45 positives across 100+ million
years of metallogenic history. We hypothesized that if Tanacross
had a single-event, single-deposit-type AOI like Arizona, DevNet
would win. Arizona's southeast porphyry belt is single deposit
type, single Laramide event, single tectonic setting. DevNet still
underperformed RF by 14 percentage points at top 1 percent capture.
**Deposit homogeneity is not the gap.**

**Hypothesis 2: feature engineering is the gap.** We applied the
published DEEP-SEAM preprocessing pipeline (isometric log-ratio
transformation plus principal-components analysis of the geochem;
gray-level co-occurrence matrix textural features on every
geophysical raster) to both Tanacross and Arizona. On Tanacross it
made RF worse by 7 percentage points at top-1; on Arizona it hurt
RF by 8 percentage points and helped DevNet by 5 percentage points,
but DevNet still trailed RF by 9 percentage points overall.
**Feature engineering is not the gap.**

**Hypothesis 3: the architecture is the differentiator.** Cell A
confirmed DevNet beats RF on the Curnamona data, so the
architecture *is* doing real work in that specific setting. But
the advantage doesn't transfer: on Tanacross and Arizona, RF wins
regardless of features. **The architecture's advantage is dataset-
specific, not algorithmic.**

### One implication established

The Curnamona result depends on the **specific dataset construction**
the original authors built, not on the architecture or feature
pipeline alone. The combination of (1) a very small positive set,
(2) a tightly curated homogeneous deposit population, (3) heavy
upstream ILR-PCA + GLCM feature engineering, and (4) the anomaly-
detection framing of the loss function produces a setup where
DevNet's deviation-from-Gaussian-prior loss can find the deposits as
genuine feature-space outliers. Standard regional MPM on raw public
data does not produce that data shape, even when we apply the same
preprocessing pipeline aimed at reproducing it.

### A geological explanation for why the architecture is mismatched

DEEP-SEAM's deviation loss tells the model to find cells that look
unusual compared to a Gaussian-distributed reference. That assumption
fits Curnamona because the upstream PCA + GLCM compression reshapes
the data into something approximately Gaussian, with the seven
deposits sitting in the tails — they really are outliers in the
compressed feature space.

The same assumption fails on regional MPM. The Tanacross and
Arizona feature stacks (and the Lawley continental cube)
deliberately encode a *geological recipe* through proximity-to-
feature columns (distance to fault, distance to passive margin,
distance to black shale) and presence-flag dictionaries (sedimentary
host, igneous host, alteration). A prospective cell is a cell that
fits the recipe — sedimentary plus near-fault plus near-black-shale
— not a cell that stands out from everything else. Gradient-boosted-
trees and Random Forest can learn the recipe through multivariate
conditional rules. DevNet, told to find the rare, finds that the
recipe is *common* across many cells and produces near-random
ordering.

## The Lawley follow-on

A separate experiment ([prospectus deep-dive](../prospectus/deep_seam_cross_region.qmd))
ran the same head-to-head comparison on Lawley et al. 2022's
continental Zn-Pb data cube — 5.16 million H3 hexagons across the
United States, Canada, and Australia, 2,027 positive MVT deposits.
Under 2-D quantile-block cross-validation, the gradient-boosted-
trees model landed 28 percent of held-out positives in its top 1
percent of cells; DevNet landed zero. The cross-continent transfer
test (train US/Canada → predict Australia) sent DevNet's AUC to
0.343 — below 0.5, meaning the predictions are actively anti-
correlated with the truth. The Lawley result is the fourth
independent piece of evidence for the same finding.

## What this implies for the field

The deep-anomaly architectures (DEEP-SEAM, DevNet, related
deviation-loss methods) are being proposed in the recent MPM
literature (*Geoscientific Model Development*, *Natural Resources
Research*) as the next step beyond classical tree-based machine
learning. The four-region experiment says, concretely, that they
are not yet ready as a drop-in replacement for Random Forest or
gradient boosting on standard regional prospectivity workflows.

The architecture has a specific niche — small-positive datasets
with heavy upstream feature decomposition that genuinely shapes the
data into anomaly form — and outside that niche they underperform
2010-vintage tree-based methods by 15 to 37 percentage points of
AUC. For an exploration company evaluating which model to deploy on
a target region, knowing where the architecture works and where it
doesn't is more useful than a blanket claim that the new method is
better.

The cleanest follow-up experiment, queued for a later milestone, is
to apply the same architecture to a *different* REE province (Mt
Weld, Bayan Obo, Bear Lodge) with the same DEEP-SEAM preprocessing
pipeline. If DevNet wins there too, the architecture has a real
class-of-problem advantage we can document with a transferability
claim. If it fails on a second REE province, the Curnamona result
is dataset-specific in an even tighter sense.

## Files and reproduction

- Curnamona reference implementation: [scripts/reproductions/deep_seam/replicate.py](../../scripts/reproductions/deep_seam/replicate.py)
- Cell A (Curnamona + DevNet): [data/derived/deep_seam_cell_a_loo.json](../../data/derived/deep_seam_cell_a_loo.json)
- Cell B (Curnamona + RF): [data/derived/deep_seam_cell_b_loo.json](../../data/derived/deep_seam_cell_b_loo.json)
- Tanacross Path 5 4-cell: [scripts/eastak/path5_oof_comparison.py](../../scripts/eastak/path5_oof_comparison.py), [data/derived/eastak/path5_decomposed_metrics.json](../../data/derived/eastak/path5_decomposed_metrics.json)
- Arizona Path 3 4-cell: [scripts/arizona/oof_comparison.py](../../scripts/arizona/oof_comparison.py), [data/derived/arizona/path3_decomposed_metrics.json](../../data/derived/arizona/path3_decomposed_metrics.json)
- Lawley continental: [scripts/reproductions/lawley/phase3_devnet.py](../../scripts/reproductions/lawley/phase3_devnet.py)
- Cross-region writeup: [research/cross_region_methodology_findings_2026-05.md](../../research/cross_region_methodology_findings_2026-05.md)
- DEEP-SEAM implementation: [src/ai_minerals/model_devnet.py](../../src/ai_minerals/model_devnet.py)

## Sources

- Pang, G. et al. 2019. Deep Anomaly Detection with Deviation Networks. arXiv:1911.08623.
- Luo, Z. et al. 2026. DEEP-SEAM: an explainable semi-supervised deep learning framework for mineral prospectivity mapping. *Geosci. Model Dev.* 19, 2593.
- Lawley, C. J. M. et al. 2022. Data-driven prospectivity modelling of sediment-hosted Zn-Pb mineral systems and their critical raw materials. *Ore Geology Reviews* 141, 104635.
- USGS, National Geochemical Database (NGDB), Alaska Geochemical Database v4 (AGDB4), State Geologic Map Compilation (SGMC), Mineral Resources Data System (MRDS).
- DARPA-CRITICALMAAS program (USGS / DARPA AI for Critical Minerals).
